skill.name: lint-and-validate
skill.description: Runs repeatable linting, type checking, and validation workflows for Node.js, TypeScript, and Python projects after code changes.
skill.summary: Validate code changes with project-aware linting and type checks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static findings for Ruby backtick execution and weak cryptography are false positives from Markdown text. The skill intentionally runs linting and type-checking commands, including project-defined npm scripts, so it should be published with a warning for untrusted repositories.

medium_findings.title: Project-Defined Commands Can Execute During Linting
medium_findings.description: The script builds linter command arrays from project metadata and runs them with subprocess.run. This avoids shell injection, but npm scripts and local tooling can execute arbitrary project-controlled code when used on untrusted repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is direct evidence of external command execution. The command arguments are fixed arrays, so the risk is execution of project-defined tooling rather than shell injection.

medium_findings.title: Skill Instructs Agents to Run Local Validation Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill tells agents to run npm, npx, TypeScript, Ruff, Bandit, MyPy, and helper scripts. These commands are normal for validation workflows, but they can execute code or dependencies from the target project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit in the skill instructions and require Bash access. They appear legitimate for the skill purpose, but still create elevated risk on untrusted codebases.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto Finding Is False Positive
low_findings.description: The referenced line is the skill description and contains the phrase static analysis. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing call, or encryption logic is present at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains metadata text only. I found no evidence of cryptographic code at the reported location.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The reported SKILL.md locations are Markdown inline command examples, not Ruby backtick execution. They are still command guidance, but they are not executable Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown, and the backtick content is rendered as inline code. There is no Ruby source file or Ruby interpreter path involved.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: subprocess.run executes linter command arrays in the target project directory. This is expected for a validation runner but risky for untrusted projects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly invokes subprocess.run. The list-form arguments reduce shell injection risk but do not remove execution risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash-Oriented Validation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill requires Bash-capable tooling and recommends commands that run package managers, linters, type checkers, and audit tools.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented. Their behavior depends on the target repository and installed dependencies.

content.value_statement: Code changes often fail because lint, type, or audit checks are skipped. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through repeatable validation.
actual_capabilities: Detects Node.js and Python project markers before choosing checks.
Runs npm lint, ESLint, TypeScript, Ruff, and MyPy when configured.
Reports pass or fail output for each configured linter.
Documents validation commands for npm, TypeScript, Python, and security checks.
Provides a type coverage helper for TypeScript and Python files.
limitations: It can execute project-defined npm scripts, so untrusted repositories need isolation.
It depends on linters and type checkers being installed in the environment.
It does not replace a full manual security review.
The type coverage helper limits file analysis for large projects.

use_cases.title: Validate Pull Request Changes
use_cases.description: Run the project lint and type checks before a pull request is marked ready.

use_cases.title: Check Generated Code
use_cases.description: Confirm that AI-assisted edits still meet local project quality rules.

use_cases.title: Review Multi-Language Repositories
use_cases.description: Apply consistent validation steps across TypeScript and Python parts of a repository.

prompt_templates.title: Run Basic Validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate the current project after my latest code change and summarize any failures.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check after a small edit

prompt_templates.title: Fix Lint Failures
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the appropriate lint checks, fix safe issues, then rerun validation until it passes or blockers remain.

prompt_templates.title: Check Types and Security
prompt_templates.prompt: Run linting, type checks, and available audit commands for this repository. Explain each failure and the safest fix.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Validation Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the repository validation setup, run available checks, identify missing lint or type coverage, and recommend improvements.

output_examples.input: Validate a TypeScript project before opening a pull request.
output_examples.output: The assistant runs lint and type checks, reports pass and fail status, and lists concrete fixes for remaining issues.

output_examples.input: Check a Python service after generated edits.
output_examples.output: The assistant runs Python linting and type checks when available, then summarizes errors by file and severity.

output_examples.input: Review a repository with no configured lint tools.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports that no configured tools were found and suggests practical setup steps.

best_practices: Run this skill after every meaningful code change.
Use an isolated environment when validating untrusted repositories.
Review command output before applying broad automated fixes.
anti_patterns: Do not run package scripts from unknown repositories on a personal machine.
Do not treat lint success as proof that code is secure.
Do not ignore type check failures because tests still pass.

faq.question: What languages does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets Node.js, TypeScript, and Python validation workflows.

faq.question: Does it automatically fix every issue?
faq.answer: No. It can guide safe fixes, but some failures need manual review.

faq.question: Can it run security checks?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents npm audit and Bandit commands when those tools fit the project.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: Use isolation for untrusted repositories because validation commands can execute project-defined code.

faq.question: Does it require existing project configuration?
faq.answer: It works best when the repository already has lint, type, or audit tools configured.

faq.question: Which assistants can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
